title: Gated Recurrent Units (GRU)
learning_outcomes: Compare the structural complexity and parameter footprint of GRUs and LSTMs.
Formulate the reset gate and update gate equations of a GRU cell.
Formulate the linear interpolation update of the hidden state and trace its gradient pathway.

content_sections.title: Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) vs. LSTMs
content_sections.content:
The **Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)**, introduced by Cho et al. in 2014, is a simplified variation of the LSTM. While LSTMs are highly effective, they are structurally complex and computationally expensive. GRUs address this by optimizing the gate layout.

### 1. Structural Simplifications
*   **Single-State Representation:** Unlike the LSTM which maintains both a cell state $\\mathbf{c}_t$ and a hidden state $\\mathbf{h}_t$, the GRU collapses them into a single hidden state vector $\\mathbf{h}_t$.
*   **Two Gates instead of Three:** GRUs eliminate the separate forget and input gates, replacing them with a single **update gate**. They also eliminate the separate output gate, replacing it with a **reset gate**.

### 2. Parameter Footprint
An LSTM requires four sets of weight matrices (forget, input, candidate, output), whereas a GRU requires only three (reset, update, candidate). Consequently, a GRU uses about **25% fewer parameters** than an LSTM of the same hidden dimension. This reduces memory footprint, mitigates overfitting risks on smaller datasets, and accelerates training loops.

callouts.title: GRU vs. LSTM Trade-off
callouts.content: Due to having fewer parameters, GRUs train faster and can outperform LSTMs on smaller or medium-sized datasets. However, on massive datasets with extremely complex, long-range dependencies, the expressive capacity of the dual-state LSTM can yield superior final accuracy.

content_sections.title: Gating Formulations in GRUs
content_sections.content:
At each time step $t$, given input $\mathbf{x}_t$ and previous hidden state $\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$, the GRU computes two gating masks and a candidate state:

### 1. Reset Gate ($r_t$)
Determines how much of the historical hidden state memory $\\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ should be ignored when computing the new candidate state:

$$\\mathbf{r}_t = \\sigma(\\mathbf{W}_{xr} \\mathbf{x}_t + \\mathbf{W}_{hr} \\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \\mathbf{b}_r)$$

If $\\mathbf{r}_t \\approx \\mathbf{0}$, the model resets the past history and acts as if reading the first token of a sequence.

### 2. Update Gate ($z_t$)
Decides what percentage of the previous hidden state to keep, and what percentage of new candidate activations to write. It acts as a combined forget and input gate:

$$\\mathbf{z}_t = \\sigma(\\mathbf{W}_{xz} \\mathbf{x}_t + \\mathbf{W}_{hz} \\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \\mathbf{b}_z)$$

### 3. Candidate Hidden State ($\tilde{h}_t$)
Computes new candidate activations, where the reset gate scales the influence of the previous hidden state:

$$\\tilde{\\mathbf{h}}_t = \\tanh(\\mathbf{W}_{xh} \\mathbf{x}_t + \\mathbf{r}_t \\odot (\\mathbf{W}_{hh} \\mathbf{h}_{t-1}) + \\mathbf{b}_h)$$

Note that the recurrent weight multiplication is applied *after* gating the hidden state.

callouts.title: Reset Gate Modulates History, Not Input
callouts.content: A common mistake is applying the reset gate $\\mathbf{r}_t$ to the current input token $\\mathbf{x}_t$ or to the sum of terms. The reset gate modulates only the previous hidden state $\\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ to filter which past details are mixed into the new candidate state $\\tilde{\\mathbf{h}}_t$.

content_sections.title: Hidden State Update & Linear Interpolation
content_sections.content:
Once the gates and candidate state are calculated, the new hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ is computed using a **linear interpolation** modulated by the update gate $\mathbf{z}_t$.

### 1. Update Equation

$$\\mathbf{h}_t = (1 - \\mathbf{z}_t) \\odot \\mathbf{h}_{t-1} + \\mathbf{z}_t \\odot \\tilde{\\mathbf{h}}_t$$

*   If the update gate $\\mathbf{z}_t \\approx \\mathbf{0}$, the hidden state remains unchanged: $\\mathbf{h}_t \\approx \\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$. This allows information to be copied forward across many steps without modification.
*   If $\\mathbf{z}_t \\approx \\mathbf{1}$, the hidden state is overwritten by the candidate: $\\mathbf{h}_t \\approx \\tilde{\\mathbf{h}}_t$.

### 2. Gradient Flow Path
Like the LSTM cell state, the GRU hidden state update is additive. The derivative of $\\mathbf{h}_t$ with respect to $\\mathbf{h}_{t-1}$ contains the term $(1 - \\mathbf{z}_t)$. If the network learns to keep the update gate closed ($1 - \\mathbf{z}_t \\approx \\mathbf{1}$), the gradient propagates backward through time without decay, mitigating vanishing gradients.
